title: Kann ich oder muss ich? (Can I or must I? - Modal Verbs: Usage)

sections.title: Introduction to Modal Verbs Usage
sections.description: In German, modal verbs usually appear in the second position of a sentence, while the main action (the infinitive) is pushed to the very end.

content.description: Modal verbs modify the meaning of the main verb in a sentence, expressing attitudes like necessity, ability, permission, or desire.

sections.title: müssen (Must / To have to)
sections.description: Expresses a necessity or an obligation where there is no alternative.

content.grammar_note: It is a "strong" obligation. If you don't do it, there are consequences.
examples: Ich muss auf die Toilette (gehen). (I must go to the toilet.)

sections.title: können (Can / To be able to)
sections.description: This verb is versatile and has three primary uses:

uses: Possibility: A chance or opportunity exists.
Permission (Informal): It isn't forbidden (similar to dürfen).
examples: Ich kann Englisch (sprechen). (I can speak English.)
Hier kann man essen und trinken. (One can eat and drink here.)
Hier kann man parken. (One can park here.)

sections.title: dürfen / nicht dürfen (May / Must not)
sections.description: dürfen: Expresses permission (It is allowed). nicht dürfen: Expresses a prohibition (It is forbidden).

content.exception_note: In English, "must not" means prohibition. In German, "muss nicht" means "don't have to" (optional), while "darf nicht" is the strict prohibition.
examples: Heute dürfen meine Kinder fernsehen. (Today my children are allowed to watch TV.)
Hier dürfen Sie nicht rauchen. (You are not allowed to smoke here.)

content.grammar_note: Technically, this is the subjunctive (Konjunktiv II) form of mögen, but it is used as its own modal verb in daily life.
examples: Ich möchte einen Kaffee (trinken), bitte. (I would like to drink a coffee, please.)

sections.title: wollen (Want)
sections.description: A direct, strong wish or intention.

content.usage_note: Using wollen with people can sound rude or demanding. Use möchten for politeness.
examples: Ich will gerne Ski-Urlaub machen. (I want to go on a skiing holiday.)

sections.title: sollen (Should / Ought to)
sections.description: This verb is often used when a requirement comes from an external source.

uses: Reporting an order: Repeating what someone else (like a doctor or boss) told you to do.
examples: Soll ich dir einen Kaffee machen? (Should I make you a coffee?)
Der Arzt sagt, ich soll die Tabletten nehmen. (The doctor says I should/am supposed to take the pills.)

sections.title: Summary Table of Usage
sections.description: Quick reference for modal verb usage contexts and English equivalents.

sections.title: Grammar & Sentence Structure Tips
sections.description: Important grammar rules and sentence structure patterns for modal verbs.

tips: The "Verb Sandwich": The modal verb is conjugated and sits in the second position. The second verb stays in its basic (infinitive) form and sits at the very end.
Example: Ich kann heute nicht kommen. (I cannot come today.)
Dropping the Main Verb: In German, if the meaning is clear (usually with verbs of motion like "to go" or "to do"), you can drop the main verb at the end.
Example: Ich muss nach Hause (gehen). -> Ich muss nach Hause.
Vowel Shifts: Most modal verbs change their stem vowel in the singular forms (ich, du, er/sie/es).
Example: können becomes ich kann; müssen becomes ich muss.
